title: Mixture of experts
description: A sparse feed-forward layer that routes each token to a small subset of expert MLPs instead of one dense block.
openingSummary: A mixture-of-experts layer routes each token through a few specialist MLPs instead of one dense feed-forward block, trading sparse activation for extra capacity.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A mixture-of-experts layer replaces the single dense feed-forward network in a transformer block with many parallel expert MLPs plus a router. For each token position, a gating network scores every expert and sends the hidden vector to only the top-k experts. The chosen expert outputs are then weighted and merged back into one updated token state.

whatItOptimizes.title: What It Optimizes
whatItOptimizes.body: MoE optimizes for parameter capacity without forcing every token to pay for every expert. Instead of making one dense FFN larger, it keeps activation sparse and lets different tokens use different expert subsets.

practicalBenefit.title: Practical Benefit
practicalBenefit.body: This is why MoE matters in large-model scaling discussions: it can grow the total parameter pool sharply while keeping active compute per token much closer to a dense baseline.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: The token state first goes through a router. The router picks a small top-k expert set for that token. Each selected expert runs its own FFN, and their outputs are blended into one result. The next token in the sequence can activate a different expert pair even though the block slot in the architecture stays the same.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: The dense FFN formula below is the baseline that activates one shared path for every token. The MoE formula adds routing and weighted expert selection to the same general slot.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: Compared with dense FFN variants, MoE changes not just the hidden path shape but the activation pattern itself. The key contrast is dense-for-every-token versus sparse-top-k routing.

variantsAndNearbyModules.title: Variants And Nearby Modules
variantsAndNearbyModules.body: These nearby pages show the dense FFN baseline and the gated dense alternative that readers usually compare against MoE before jumping into sparse expert routing details.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: Large decoder-only language models often mention expert count and top-k routing in their architecture summaries when they adopt MoE blocks.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: MoE adds routing, capacity management, and load-balancing concerns that dense FFNs do not have. It can raise total capacity efficiently, but the training and serving story becomes more complex.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: Sparse expert routing remains one of the clearest ways to separate total parameter count from active per-token compute, so readers keep encountering MoE whenever scaling tradeoffs come up.

computeFlow.alt: Comparison between a standard dense FFN and a mixture-of-experts layer with top-k routing
computeFlow.caption: Switch between the dense baseline and MoE to see how routing replaces one shared MLP with a sparse expert pool

comparisonTable.caption: How mixture of experts compares with nearby FFN variants

moe.pathShape: Router selects a few expert MLPs, then merges them
moe.activeCompute: Only top-k experts activate for each token
moe.mainTradeoff: Adds much more parameter capacity, but routing and balancing add system complexity

standard.pathShape: One dense expand -> activate -> project path
standard.activeCompute: All hidden units in one shared MLP
standard.mainTradeoff: Simple dense baseline, but every token pays for the whole block

swiglu.pathShape: Value branch multiplied by a SiLU gate branch
swiglu.activeCompute: All hidden units plus a learned gate
swiglu.mainTradeoff: More expressive dense path, but still no sparse expert capacity

ht.definition: Hidden state for token position t.

w1.definition: Projection into the wide hidden layer.

sigma.definition: Pointwise activation inside the dense hidden layer.

ht.definition: Hidden state for token position t.

r.definition: Router that scores experts for the current token.

topk.definition: Operation that keeps only the highest-scoring expert choices.

alpha.definition: Weight assigned to expert e for this token.

expert.definition: Expert-specific FFN chosen by the router.

inactiveExperts.label: Other experts stay inactive for this token
